attributes.stem:
Last week, a 31-year old-woman presented to her GP with an eight week history of amenorrhoea. The urine b-hCG was positive and ultrasound confirmed an intrauterine pregnancy, crown-rump length (CRL) measuring 20mm. The fetal heartbeat was present. All other examination findings were normal.

Today, the patient presents to the emergency department with an 15 hour history of significant vaginal bleeding and abdominal cramping. On history, estimated blood loss is approximately 400ml. Speculum examination demonstrates an open os, with moderate pooling of blood in the posterior fornix. Products of conception (POC) can be seen in the cervical canal and these are gently removed at the time of examination. Bimanual examination demonstrates a soft and central uterus approximately 7/40 size. Ultrasound demonstrates retained POC (18x12x15mm).

On presentation her blood pressure is 110/62 mmHg, pulse 120/min, temperature 37.8oC, respiratory rate 18/min and SpO2 99% on room air. Her capillary refill is 3 seconds. Two hours later her blood pressure has dropped to 96/54 mmHg and her pulse risen to 125/min. There is ongoing vaginal bleeding.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has presented with an incomplete miscarriage. Diagnosis of this is based on the history of a viable intrauterine pregnancy one week previously, ongoing vaginal bleeding, open os and presence of products of conception in the cervical canal. 

While expectant or medical management of an incomplete miscarriage is not unreasonable, this patient presents with significant bleeding and haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension). As such, the most appropriate management, combined with appropriate fluid resuscitation, is to go to theatre for a suction dilation and curettage. Suction dilation and curettage is a surgical procedure performed under general anaesthetic where the cervix is dilated and the endometrial lining and associated tissue/POC are scraped away from the uterus and removed.

Expectant management involves ensuring patient comfort, education and counselling around the loss of pregnancy, and aims to resolve the miscarriage without medical intervention. Expectant management is appropriate for patients who are experiencing a miscarriage who are medically stable, and do not wish to use medical or surgical options. If a patient has significant bleeding, or demonstrated deterioration in their vitals, medical or surgical management may be more appropriate.

Misoprostol/mifepristone is used for medical termination. Misoprostol is often used for medical management of retained products and is appropriate in patients who do not wish to undertake expectant management, are not showing signs of deterioration, or who have a missed miscarriage. Mifepristone (an anti-progesterone agent) should be given orally, followed by sublingual misoprostol (a prostaglandin analogue) after 48 hours.

Methotrexate is a cytotoxic drug which interrupts the function of folic acid to stop cell division and proliferation. It is used in those with a confirmed ectopic pregnancy less than 5cm diameter who are hemodynamically stable, with a serum b-hCG less than 5000mlU/ml (2). The tissue is then absorbed into the lining of the fallopian tube. In a patient with an ectopic pregnancy outside of these parameters, laparoscopy and removal may be required.

Emergency laparoscopy for salpingectomy/salpingostomy is used in patients with a confirmed ectopic pregnancy located in the fallopian tube who are hemodynamically unstable, demonstrate symptoms and signs of ectopic rupture, are otherwise medically unable to tolerate methotrexate. In hemodynamically stable patients who are not candidates for medical management, surgery should only occur if there is evidence of a tubal/adnexal mass suggestive of ectopic pregnancy on TVUS examination.
learningPoints: Dilation and curettage is appropriate management in a patient who has been diagnosed with an incomplete miscarriage and demonstrates signs of haemodynamic instability and ongoing bleeding.
references: Chu J, Devall A, Beeson L, Hardy P, Cheed V, Sun Y et al. Mifepristone and misoprostol versus misoprostol alone for the management of missed miscarriage (MifeMiso): a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. The Lancet. 2020;396(10253):770-778.

Tulandi T. Ectopic pregnancy: choosing a treatment. In: Scheiber C A, Chakrabarti A, editors. UpToDate [Internet]. Waltham (MA): UpToDate Inc, September 10 2021 [Cited: January 12 2022]. Available from: https://www.uptodate.com.acs.hcn.com.au/contents/ectopic-pregnancy-choosing-a-treatment?search=ectopic%20pregnancy&source=search_result&selectedTitle=3~150&usage_type=default&display_rank=3#H3021183296D/C indications

Trinder J, Brocklehurst P, Porter R. Management of Miscarriage: Expectant, Medical, or Surgical? Results of Randomised Controlled Trial (Miscarriage Treatment [MIST] Trial). Obstetrics & Gynecology. 2006;108(2):450.
